title: API Authentication Guide: API Keys, OAuth 2.0, JWT & Bearer
description: Did you know 63% of APIs use the wrong auth method? Learn the exact differences between API Keys, OAuth 2.0, Bearer Tokens.

schemas.headline: API Authentication Guide: API Keys, OAuth 2.0, JWT & Bearer Tokens
schemas.description: Learn the exact differences between API Keys, OAuth 2.0, Bearer Tokens, and JWT, and discover which api authentication methods you should use.

mainEntity.name: What are the most common API authentication methods?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common API authentication methods include Basic Authentication (username/password), API Keys (a single static token), Bearer Tokens (dynamic tokens passed in the Authorization header), OAuth 2.0 (a delegated authorization framework), and JWT (JSON Web Tokens, a specific stateless token format often used as a Bearer token).

OAuth 2.0 is like a hotel keycard system. Instead of giving a valet your car keys (your actual password), you give them a temporary, limited-access pass (an access token). It allows third-party applications to access a user's data without ever seeing their actual password, by having the user log in directly with the provider (like Google or GitHub) who then issues the token.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between an API key vs bearer token?

acceptedAnswer.text: An API key is typically a long-lived, static string used to identify the project or developer making the request, often passed in a URL parameter or custom header. A Bearer token is usually short-lived, dynamic (often generated upon login via OAuth or a similar flow), identifies a specific user, and is strictly passed in the HTTP 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' header.

mainEntity.name: How to authenticate API requests securely?

acceptedAnswer.text: To authenticate API requests securely, always use HTTPS to encrypt traffic. Avoid placing tokens or API keys in the URL (query parameters) where they can be logged. Instead, use standard HTTP headers like 'Authorization'. For machine-to-machine communication, use API keys or Client Credentials. For user-facing apps, use OAuth 2.0 or JWT-based Bearer tokens, and implement token expiration and rotation.

mainEntity.name: When should I use JWT over session cookies for APIs?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use JWTs when building stateless APIs, mobile applications, or microservices where you don't want to look up a session in a database on every request. Because JWTs contain their own claims and signature, the server can verify them instantly. Use session cookies for traditional monolithic server-side rendered web applications where you need strict control over invalidating sessions instantly.
